specification.topic-statement: The PostgreSQL wrapper seam layer provides build-mode-controlled substitution points for seven libpq operations, enabling test code to replace any operation with a controlled implementation without modifying production call sites.

scope.in-scope: The seven wrapped libpq operations, the dual-build mechanism that selects between inlined and overridable implementations, the weak-symbol override protocol for test code, and coverage exclusion for the debug-build bodies.
scope.boundary: The actual PostgreSQL connection lifecycle, schema migration, transaction control, and teardown are covered by the Database Connection specification. This specification covers only the wrapper seam mechanism.

The behavior of the seven underlying libpq functions — query execution, result inspection, socket access, input consumption, and notification polling — is defined entirely by the PostgreSQL client library. The wrapper seam adds no logic; it either delegates unconditionally (debug) or is eliminated entirely (release).

What test-supplied override functions do with their arguments, what values they return, and how they interact with the rest of a test are concerns of individual tests. This specification covers only the seam mechanism that makes overriding possible.

data-contracts.contract: The seven wrapped operations and their pass-through signatures:

behavior.release-build-inlining:
When the project is compiled with the NDEBUG flag set, all seven wrappers are defined as static inline functions directly in the header. Each wrapper body is a single unconditional delegation to the corresponding libpq function, passing all arguments through without modification and returning the result unchanged. Because the functions are inline, the compiler eliminates them entirely; production binaries contain no wrapper call overhead and no additional symbol for any of the seven operations.
In release builds the header file contains the complete function bodies, not just declarations. This is intentional: the inline keyword requires the definition to be visible at each call site. No separate compilation unit is involved for release builds.
behavior.debug-build-weak-symbols:
When NDEBUG is not set (debug and test builds), the seven wrappers are compiled as weak symbols from a dedicated compilation unit. The header exposes only declarations in this mode. Weak symbols allow a linker to accept a strong-symbol override from any other translation unit linked into the same binary. If no override is present, the linker uses the weak definition, which delegates unconditionally to the corresponding libpq function, identical in behavior to the release inline.
The weak-symbol approach is chosen over function pointers or dependency injection structs because it requires no changes at call sites and imposes no runtime overhead in builds where overrides are absent.
Test overrides work by providing a strong-symbol definition of the same name in the test translation unit. The linker selects the strong symbol over the weak one at link time. This means overrides are per-binary, not per-call-site, and a single override applies to all callers within the linked test binary.
behavior.coverage-exclusion: The debug-build compilation unit is bracketed by coverage exclusion markers. The seven function bodies inside it are excluded from line coverage measurement. Because each body is a trivial single-line delegation with no branches, including them would inflate denominator counts without providing meaningful coverage signal. All meaningful coverage data for these operations comes from the call sites and from override implementations in test files, which are not excluded.
behavior.override-protocol:
A test that needs to control one of the seven operations provides a strong-symbol function definition with the identical name and signature as the wrapped operation. No registration, configuration, or pointer assignment is required. The override is active for the entire duration of the test binary's execution; there is no mechanism to swap overrides between individual test cases within the same binary.
Overrides may return synthetic result handles, error codes, or any other value appropriate to the scenario being tested. Because the underlying libpq is never called when an override is present, no database connection is required to exercise error handling paths that depend on these operations.
Certain libpq functions that are not themselves in the wrapper set — such as the error-message retrieval function, the result-clearing function, the row-count function, and the null-check function — are overridden by test code using strong-symbol definitions of the libpq names themselves rather than wrapper names. These functions were not included in the wrapper set, so tests that need to control them bypass the wrapper layer entirely and override the library symbol. This is a distinct override strategy from the one used for the seven wrapped operations.
behavior.unsafe-function-ban: The debug-build compilation unit includes a compile-time enforcement header as its final include. This header poisons a set of unsafe C standard library and string functions via a compiler pragma, causing any use of those banned symbols within the unit to be a compile error. None of the seven wrapper bodies use any banned function; the inclusion is a project-wide discipline applied uniformly to all implementation units.
